function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
Using only disclosed KPIs, segment revenue, and Segment Adjusted EBITDA, summarize the primary investment narrative for Space, Connectivity, and AI. Which segment’s equity story relies most heavily on forward expectations rather than presently disclosed economics?

steps.reasoning_content:
The user is asking about SpaceX's S-1 filing, specifically about the investment narrative for three segments: Space, Connectivity, and AI. I need to:

1. Retrieve the S-1 filing
2. Find KPIs, segment revenue, and Segment Adjusted EBITDA for each segment
3. Analyze which segment's equity story relies most heavily on forward expectations

Let me start by parsing the S-1 filing.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

steps.reasoning_content: Let me retrieve information about the segments, KPIs, revenue, and Segment Adjusted EBITDA from the S-1 filing.

arguments.prompt: What are the three reportable segments (Space, Connectivity, AI) and their disclosed KPIs, segment revenue, and Segment Adjusted EBITDA figures for the most recent fiscal year and any prior periods presented in {{spacex_s1}}? Please include specific dollar amounts and any disclosed operational metrics like launches, payloads, Starlink subscribers, active satellites, xAI revenue, GPU clusters, or training compute.

What does the S-1 filing {{spacex_s1}} disclose about the AI segment (xAI), including revenue, Segment Adjusted EBITDA, GPU cluster capacity, Grok product, training compute, customer concentration, and any forward-looking statements about commercialization or monetization?

results.content:
Based on the provided financial highlights, here are the three reportable segments along with their disclosed KPIs, segment revenue, and Segment Adjusted EBITDA figures for the most recent fiscal year (FY 2025), prior periods (FY 2024, FY 2023), and the three months ended March 31, 2026 (Q1 2026):

### 1. Space Segment
**Financial Performance:**
*   **Segment Revenue:** $619 million (Q1 2026), $4,086 million (FY 2025), $3,796 million (FY 2024), $3,557 million (FY 2023)
*   **Segment Adjusted EBITDA:** $(351) million (Q1 2026), $653 million (FY 2025). *(Note: FY 2024 and FY 2023 Adjusted EBITDA are not explicitly stated as totals in the text, though components like D&A and SBC are provided).*

**Disclosed KPIs & Operational Metrics:**
*   **Starship R&D Expense:** $930 million in Q1 2026 and $3,004 million in FY 2025.
*   **Capital Expenditures:** $1,052 million (Q1 2026), $3,832 million (FY 2025), $2,032 million (FY 2024), $1,497 million (FY 2023).
*   **Launches:** While a "Launches (number)" line item is referenced in a table, the specific numerical values are cut off in the provided text. The narrative notes that Q1 2026 saw a revenue decrease due to "lower Launch Services missions" and "customer launches."

### 2. Connectivity Segment
**Financial Performance:**
*   **Segment Revenue:** $3,257 million (Q1 2026), $11,387 million (FY 2025), $7,599 million (FY 2024), $3,869 million (FY 2023)
*   **Segment Adjusted EBITDA:** $2,087 million (Q1 2026), $7,168 million (FY 2025), $3,849 million (FY 2024), $1,602 million (FY 2023)

**Disclosed KPIs & Operational Metrics:**
*   **Starlink Subscribers:** 10.3 million (Q1 2026), 8.9 million (FY 2025), 4.4 million (FY 2024), 2.3 million (FY 2023). *(Also 5.0 million in Q1 2025).*
*   **Starlink ARPU (Average Revenue Per User):** $66/month (Q1 2026), $81/month (FY 2025), $91/month (FY 2024), $99/month (FY 2023). *(Also $86/month in Q1 2025).*
*   **Active Satellites:** Approximately 9,600 Starlink broadband and mobile satellites in Low-Earth Orbit as of March 31, 2026.
*   **Capital Expenditures:** $1,332 million (Q1 2026), $4,178 million (FY 2025), $3,498 million (FY 2024), $2,455 million (FY 2023).

### 3. AI Segment
**Financial Performance:**
*   **Segment Revenue:** $818 million (Q1 2026), $3,201 million (FY 2025), $2,620 million (FY 2024), $2,961 million (FY 2023)
*   **Segment Adjusted EBITDA:** $(609) million (Q1 2026), $(1,237) million (FY 2025), $347 million (FY 2024), $1,222 million (FY 2023)

**Disclosed KPIs & Operational Metrics:**
*   **Nameplate Compute Draw:** 1.0 gigawatt (Q1 2026), 0.8 gigawatts (FY 2025), 0.3 gigawatts (FY 2024), 0 gigawatts (FY 2023). *(Also 0.3 gigawatts in Q1 2025).* The text notes the increase to 1.0 GW in Q1 2026 was driven by bringing the COLOSSUS and COLOSSUS II data centers online.
*   **Capital Expenditures:** $7,723 million (Q1 2026), $12,727 million (FY 2025), $5,633 million (FY 2024), $463 million (FY 2023).
*   **xAI Acquisition & Revenue:** xAI was acquired in February 2026 and forms the basis of the AI segment. The segment's revenue is derived from advertising, Grok and X subscriptions, and data licensing arrangements. Specific "xAI revenue" is not broken out separately from the overall AI segment financials.
*   **GPU/Compute Infrastructure:** The text highlights increased R&D expenses driven by the "depreciation of GPU hardware" and "cost of cloud computing and data center infrastructure expenses" as a result of AI data center expansions.

# AI Segment (xAI) Disclosures in the S-1 Filing

## 1. Revenue

| Period | Revenue |
|---|---|
| Q1 2026 (three months ended March 31, 2026) | **$818 million** |
| Full year 2025 | **$3,201 million** |
| Q1 2025 (three months ended March 31, 2025) | Implied ~$727 million (based on the disclosed $91 million YoY increase) |

Revenue increased **$91 million** year-over-year in Q1 2026, driven by **higher X and Grok subscriptions**.

### Revenue Recognition
The filing breaks AI revenue into three streams:
- **Subscription revenue** — recognized ratably over the subscription term (month-to-month up to one year)
- **Data licensing revenue** — recognized ratably over the period (month-to-month up to two years) as the customer consumes and benefits from the licensed data
- **API access to Grok models** — recognized ratably over the contract term (typically month-to-month or up to one year) for stand-ready access, or as services are consumed for usage-based arrangements

---

## 2. Segment Adjusted EBITDA and Loss from Operations

| Period | Loss from Operations | Segment Adjusted EBITDA |
|---|---|---|
| Q1 2026 | **$(2,469) million** | **$(609) million** |
| Q1 2025 | $(936) million | $(112) million |
| Full year 2025 | **$(6,355) million** | **$(1,237) million** |
| Full year 2024 | $(1,561) million | $347 million |
| Full year 2023 | $(3,973) million | $1,222 million |

The filing attributes the widening losses to the segment's **"earlier stage of development and continued investments to support long-term growth opportunities in AI."** Notably, the segment was EBITDA-positive in 2023 and 2024 before turning negative in 2025 and Q1 2026, coinciding with the massive ramp in compute infrastructure spending.

Segment Adjusted EBITDA is disclosed as a **non-GAAP measure**, with a cross-reference to the MD&A section for reconciliations to segment income (loss) from operations (the most directly comparable GAAP measure).

---

## 3. GPU Cluster Capacity and Compute Infrastructure

### Nameplate Compute Draw

| Period | Nameplate Compute Draw (gigawatts) |
|---|---|
| Q1 2026 | **1.0 GW** |
| Q1 2025 | 0.3 GW |
| Full year 2025 | 0.8 GW |
| Full year 2024 | 0.3 GW |
| Full year 2023 | 0 GW |

### COLOSSUS and COLOSSUS II Data Centers
- **COLOSSUS** (Memphis, Tennessee): First cluster brought online in **122 days**, repurposing the shell of an existing factory
- **COLOSSUS II**: First cluster brought online even faster — **91 days**
- Industry benchmark for comparison: ~**2 years** for a 100 MW greenfield data center
- Together, COLOSSUS and COLOSSUS II collectively provide approximately **1.0 gigawatt of compute power**, with additional power capacity available for data center operations
- COLOSSUS II achieved **construction costs considerably lower than industry benchmarks on a per-megawatt basis**
- COLOSSUS II is capable of operating entirely on **self-built behind-the-meter gigawatt-scale natural gas power generation**

### Market Opportunity Sizing
The filing quantifies the AI compute infrastructure market:
- AI workload demand corresponds to **104 million GPUs required**
- Applied assumptions: **80% utilization rate** (per National Electrical Installation Standards) and a **GPU rental rate of $3.33/hour** (per Silicon Data, based on the median of neocloud GPU rental rates in 2025)
- The filing notes the rental rate has historically varied subject to market conditions

### Capital Expenditures

| Period | AI Segment CapEx |
|---|---|
| Q1 2026 | **$7,723 million** |
| 2025 | Not fully stated in the provided chunks (Space was $3,832M; Connectivity was $4,178M; AI figure was cut off) |

The AI segment's Q1 2026 CapEx of $7.7 billion dramatically exceeds the Space ($1,052M) and Connectivity ($1,332M) segments, underscoring the capital-intensive nature of the buildout.

---

## 4. Grok Product Family

### Model Development Timeline
- **Grok-1**: Launched November 2023
- **Four major versions** released since launch, culminating in **Grok-4.3 (April 2026)**
- **Grok 5** currently in development, expected to further expand reasoning fidelity, multimodal integration, and domain-specific performance
- Described as achieving **"one of the fastest iteration cycles in the industry"**

### Performance Benchmarks
- Within **two years** of its initial model release, Grok achieved **frontier-level performance in scientific reasoning**, as measured by its **GPQA Diamond score** (an industry benchmark evaluating AI models on standardized questions written and validated by experts)
- Achieved this milestone **on a faster timeline than reported by other leading model providers**

### Parameter Scaling
- Ongoing training of next-generation models is expected to scale toward **multiple trillions of parameters**
- The filing states this "could represent a step change in reasoning depth and overall intelligence"
- Larger parameter counts are described as enabling the model to capture more complex relationships, store greater amounts of knowledge, and achieve higher reasoning capability

### Product Offerings Defined
The filing formally defines several Grok-related products:
- **Grok**: The family of frontier models, described as "a core pillar of our mission to advance humanity's understanding of the universe through the development of truth-seeking artificial intelligence"
- **Grok API**: Application programming interface enabling developers to access and integrate Grok models into external software applications and workflows
- **Grok Business**: Subscription-based offering providing organizations with access to Grok models and related tools for internal business applications
- **Grok Enterprise**: Subscription-based offering for organizations, designed for enterprise deployment
- **Grok Voice**: The Grok real-time speech engine
- **Imagine**: The company's image and video generation system

### Truth-Seeking Mission
Grok is described as a **"truth-seeking AI model"** built on Elon Musk's mission to enable humanity to understand the universe. The filing defines truth-seeking as:
> "the active, relentless pursuit of what is objectively true about reality, and grounded in evidence, logic, empirical data, and first principles thinking."

### X Platform Integration
- Grok benefits from integration with **X** (the real-time information, entertainment, and free speech platform), which serves as a **"foundational distribution and data engine"** for the AI ecosystem
- Real-time data integration through X enables **dynamic awareness of current events and user discourse** (subject to some limitations for certain content)
- Grok enhances the X ecosystem by improving content understanding, personalization, and recommendation systems, thereby increasing user engagement and platform intelligence
- Plans to **further embed Grok throughout the X platform** to enhance discovery, analysis of posts, user support, and personalization

---

## 5. Training Compute and R&D Expenses

### R&D Expense Drivers
- AI segment R&D expenses **mainly relate to the training of Grok**, development/build/testing of next-generation AI-enabled products, and data center costs to train AI-enabled products
- Costs include **cloud computing expenses**, employee compensation (salaries, benefits, share-based compensation), and **depreciation of GPU hardware**
- Q1 2026 R&D expense increased by **$1,471 million** YoY, driven by **depreciation of GPU hardware** and cloud computing/data center infrastructure expenses from AI data center expansions

### Cost of Revenue Components
AI segment cost of revenue includes:
- **Infrastructure costs** (data center facilities, lease/hosting, support, maintenance, energy, bandwidth, depreciation of servers and networking equipment, public cloud hosting, employee compensation)
- Revenue share expenses
- Payment processor fees
- Payments to creators
- Amortization of acquired intangible assets
- Allocated labor and overhead costs

### Vertical Integration as Strategic Differentiator
The filing emphasizes that the accelerated rate of innovation "stems from our highly vertically integrated stack: full ownership of training infrastructure, access to" manufacturing, data center infrastructure, and power generation. It states:
> "Without large-scale, power-efficient infrastructure, AGI cannot be deployed broadly or economically—making such infrastructure a critical strategic differentiator."

---

## 6. Customer Concentration

The provided chunks do **not** disclose specific customer concentration metrics (e.g., percentage of revenue from top customers) for the AI segment. The filing references three broad customer categories:
- **Consumer** subscribers (X and Grok subscriptions)
- **Enterprise** customers (Grok Business, Grok Enterprise, API access)
- **Government** customers

The filing mentions a strategic objective of **"Growing Enterprise and Government Adoption of Our AI Offerings"** but does not quantify current concentration in the disclosed portions.

---

## 7. Forward-Looking Statements on Commercialization and Monetization

### Growth and Investment Priority
> "In our newly acquired AI segment, we plan to prioritize growth and investment to capture significant opportunities in AI applications and compute infrastructure."

### Cost of Revenue Trajectory
> "We expect AI cost of revenue to increase in absolute dollars as we grow our revenue, and to decrease as a percentage of revenue as we monetize our products and as we expand our service offerings for AI solutions."

### Consumer AI Subscriptions
- The filing identifies consumer AI subscriptions as a significant market opportunity, driven by demand for AI tools that enhance productivity, creativity, personalization, and real-time assistance
- Grok's integration with X and proprietary access to real-time data inflows is expected to **"better address a broader set of high-frequency, high-value consumer use cases and increase user engagement and willingness to pay, positioning Grok to capture a larger share of the consumer AI"** market
- Multi-trillion parameter models in the roadmap "could represent a step change in reasoning depth and overall intelligence"

### X as "Everything App"
- Plans to evolve X into an **"Everything App"** integrating real-time information, communications, media, payments, banking, commerce, and more
- **X Money** (launched beta November 2025): payments and financial services
- **X Chat** (released November 2025): end-to-end encrypted messaging with no connection to advertising
- Further Grok embedding throughout the platform to **"reduce friction for users to adopt paid features"**

### Enterprise and Government Adoption
The filing explicitly flags **"Growing Enterprise and Government Adoption of Our AI Offerings"** as a future growth driver, though specific targets are not quantified in the disclosed portions.

### AGI Convergence
> "We believe AI is rapidly converging toward AGI, where human cognitive capabilities can be replicated and scaled at machine speeds, profoundly augmenting human productivity. Once an AGI system exists, its true value derives from the ability to create limitless duplicates of human-like intelligence, necessitating vast computational resources and cost-efficient deployment to achieve meaningful scale."

### Orbital and Lunar AI Compute
- The company expects to **leverage rockets and satellites for massive orbital deployments of AI infrastructure**, helping develop and monetize Grok faster than competitors dependent on finite Earth-based power
- If a **lunar presence** is achieved, it will potentially enable **terawatt-scale annual AI compute growth**, support deeper space exploration and industrialization, and serve as a stepping stone to Mars civilization
- The next trillion-dollar market is identified as **AI compute**

### Acquisition Context
- **xAI was founded in 2023 and acquired by SpaceX in early 2026**, making it an integral pillar of the vertically integrated company
- The AI segment is described as **"newly acquired"** throughout the filing

---

## Summary Table

| Metric | Q1 2026 | FY 2025 | FY 2024 | FY 2023 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Revenue | $818M | $3,201M | — | — |
| Loss from Operations | $(2,469)M | $(6,355)M | $(1,561)M | $(3,973)M |
| Segment Adjusted EBITDA | $(609)M | $(1,237)M | $347M | $1,222M |
| Nameplate Compute Draw | 1.0 GW | 0.8 GW | 0.3 GW | 0 GW |
| Capital Expenditures | $7,723M | — | — | — |

steps.message: I have substantial information. Let me retrieve additional details on the Space and Connectivity segments to ensure I have a complete picture of their equity stories and forward-looking statements.
steps.reasoning_content: I have a lot of information now. Let me also retrieve more details about the Space segment and Connectivity segment to make sure I have a complete picture, especially around forward-looking statements and the equity story for each.

arguments.prompt: What does the S-1 filing {{spacex_s1}} disclose about the Space segment, including launch services revenue, Starship development, government contracts (NSSL, HLS), Falcon 9 launch cadence, mission count, and any forward-looking statements about Mars, Starship commercialization, and future launch market opportunity?

What does the S-1 filing {{spacex_s1}} disclose about the Connectivity segment (Starlink), including subscriber growth, ARPU trends, direct-to-cell mobile service, enterprise/government revenue, satellite manufacturing, and any forward-looking statements about market opportunity, satellite capacity, and revenue growth?

results.content:
Based on the provided S-1 filing excerpts, here is a detailed breakdown of what the document discloses regarding the Space segment, Starship development, government contracts, launch metrics, and forward-looking market opportunities:

### 1. Launch Services Revenue & Financial Performance
*   **Recent Financials:** For the three months ended March 31, 2026, the Space segment generated revenue of $619 million, a loss from operations of $(662) million, and Segment Adjusted EBITDA of $(351) million. For the full year 2025, the segment generated revenue of $4,086 million, a loss from operations of $(657) million, and Segment Adjusted EBITDA of $653 million.
*   **Revenue Breakdown:** Space revenue is split between **Launch Services** (point-in-time recognition for deploying payloads using Falcon 9, Falcon Heavy) and **Launch & Development** (over-time recognition for developing spacecraft and providing mission services for government programs using Falcon 9, Falcon Heavy, Starship, and Dragon). 
*   **Revenue Mix Trends:** In Q1 2026, Launch Services accounted for 53.3% of Space revenue, while Launch & Development accounted for 46.7%. The company expects Launch & Development to represent a larger portion of Space revenue in the future as it serves more long-term government contracts.
*   **Inter-segment Allocation:** The Space segment only recognizes revenue from customer launches. For internal Starlink satellite launches, no inter-segment revenue is recognized; instead, those launch costs are capitalized within the Connectivity segment. The company also expects to allocate a significant amount of future launch capacity to its AI segment. 
*   **EBITDA Declines:** Space Segment Adjusted EBITDA decreased by $501 million in 2025 (down to $653 million from $1,154 million in 2024). This decline was primarily driven by accelerated investments in the Starship vehicle and launch facilities, partially offset by increased revenue from NASA Cargo Resupply Services (CRS) and a U.S. Department of War contract.

### 2. Starship Development & Commercialization
*   **R&D Investments:** The Space segment funded $930 million in R&D for Starship in Q1 2026, and $3,004 million for the full year 2025. R&D expenses are expected to increase in absolute dollars and as a percentage of revenue in 2026.
*   **Vehicle Capabilities:** Starship is described as the world’s first fully, rapidly reusable launch vehicle. The next-generation Starship V3 is designed to carry 100 metric tons to Earth’s orbit in a reusable configuration, with future generations (potentially as soon as V4) designed to reach 200 metric tons.
*   **Testing Milestones:** To date, SpaceX has executed 11 Starship flight tests. A 12th flight test is scheduled, which will debut the next-generation Starship vehicle and Super Heavy booster, powered by the next evolution of the Raptor engine, launching from a newly designed pad at Starbase. Innovative milestones include booster catches using “chopstick” arms.
*   **Commercialization Timeline & Cost Capitalization:** Starship is expected to commence payload delivery to orbit in the second half of 2026. Once commercialized (delivering payload to orbit), Starship costs will generally be capitalized and depreciated in the cost of revenue of the segment associated with the payload, rather than being expensed as R&D. 

### 3. Government Contracts (NSSL, NASA, DoD)
*   **Primary U.S. Government Provider:** SpaceX is the primary launch provider for the U.S. government. In 2025, it launched 11 of 12 National Security Space Launch (“NSSL”) medium and heavy lift missions and all five U.S. crew and cargo missions to the International Space Station for NASA.
*   **NSSL Phase 3:** U.S. national security customers have awarded approximately $13.7 billion across the NSSL Program’s Phase 3 Lane 2 contracts through 2032, supporting approximately 54 missions from 2025 to 2032. The overall Phase 3 manifest nearly doubles Phase 2’s manifest to 84 missions.
*   **Government Spending:** According to the Space Foundation, excluding classified spending, U.S. Government space spending in 2024 totaled approximately $77 billion.

### 4. Falcon 9 Launch Cadence & Mission Count
*   **Historical Scale:** As of March 31, 2026, SpaceX had completed approximately 650 orbital space launches, with over 540 completed by flight-proven Falcon rockets. Falcon 9 has approximately 620 orbital launches and an over 99% mission success rate.
*   **2025 Metrics:** In 2025 alone, SpaceX completed 170 missions across Falcon and Starship vehicles, including 165 Falcon 9 launches (157 flight-proven) and 159 flight-proven booster launches. The company launched over 2,200 metric tons, representing over 80% of mass to orbit for the world in 2025.
*   **Q1 2026 vs. Q1 2025:** Falcon launches totaled 40 in Q1 2026 (39 flight-proven) compared to 36 in Q1 2025. Customer launches decreased from 12 to 7, while internal launches increased from 24 to 33.
*   **Annual Cadence:** Falcon launches for 2023, 2024, and 2025 totaled 96, 134, and 165, respectively. Customer launches for those years were 33, 45, and 43, while internal launches were 63, 89, and 122.
*   **Future Cadence:** While Falcon 9 currently drives the majority of launch activity (averaging 13 metric tons per mission), the company expects Falcon 9 launches to decrease over time as Starship becomes a larger contributor to launch volume.

### 5. Forward-Looking Market Opportunities & Mars Vision
*   **Space Economy Growth:** Driven by reduced launch costs, active maneuverable satellites in orbit have grown from less than 1,000 in 2015 to approximately 12,700 as of March 31, 2026 (SpaceX operates ~75% of them). Payload launched to orbit grew from ~220 metric tons in 2012 to ~2,600 metric tons in 2025.
*   **Internal Growth Absorption:** Space revenue growth is expected to be lower than total company revenue growth because internal businesses (Connectivity and AI) will continue to absorb most of the growth in launch capacity.
*   **Starship-Enabled Markets:** Over the long term, SpaceX expects Starship to unlock entirely new categories of missions, including:
    *   **Point-to-point terrestrial travel:** Ultra-fast long-haul Earth transport for passengers and cargo.
    *   **Space tourism:** Increasing interest in human space travel.
    *   **In-orbit manufacturing:** Leveraging microgravity to produce materials, pharmaceuticals, and advanced components.
    *   **Multiplanetary Transport:** Large-scale passenger and cargo missions to the Moon and Mars to establish permanent human settlements.
    *   **Lunar/Martian Infrastructure:** Energy production (large-scale solar) and manufacturing capabilities on the Moon and Mars to support a self-sustaining multiplanetary civilization.
*   **Dependency Risks:** The S-1 cautions that if Starship development is delayed, it will impede the deployment of next-generation V3 satellites, V2 Mobile satellites, and orbital AI compute infrastructure, as current Falcon rockets are not capable of deploying these next-gen assets.

Based on the provided S-1 filing excerpts, here is what SpaceX discloses about its Connectivity segment (Starlink), broken down by the key areas you requested:

### 1. Subscriber Growth
*   **Massive Scale and Rapid Growth:** As of March 31, 2026, Starlink had approximately 10.3 million subscribers, up 105% from 5.0 million a year prior. For the full year 2025, subscribers grew to 8.9 million (up 99.9% from 4.4 million in 2024, which itself was up from 2.3 million in 2023).
*   **Counting Methodology:** "Starlink Subscribers" are counted on a per-Service Line basis at the end of a period. It includes both Personal (Residential, Roam) and Business (Local Priority, Global Priority) plans, but excludes managed enterprise and government contracts. 
*   **Underlying User Base:** Because multiple people can share a single Service Line (e.g., a household), the actual number of individual end-users is "meaningfully higher" than the 10.3 million reported.
*   **Global Reach:** Subscribers span 164 countries, territories, and markets, representing a small fraction of an estimated 3.3 billion potential end users in those markets.

### 2. ARPU Trends
*   **Declining ARPU:** Starlink Subscriber ARPU (Average Revenue Per User) has steadily declined: $99 in 2023, $91 in 2024, $81 in 2025, and $66 in Q1 2026 (down 22.9% from $86 in Q1 2025).
*   **Drivers of Decline:** The drop is primarily attributed to international expansion and the introduction of lower-priced service plans.
*   **Strategic Priority:** SpaceX explicitly states its strategy prioritizes driving sustainable revenue growth, expanding margins through operational efficiencies, and expanding global connectivity/market access—rather than prioritizing ARPU increases. The company expects ARPU to continue declining over the next few years as its subscriber base outside North America grows.

### 3. Direct-to-Cell Mobile Service (Starlink Mobile)
*   **Current Capabilities:** The "V1" Mobile constellation consists of approximately 650 satellites in orbit. It currently enables light data, text messaging (SMS), and over-the-top voice services (e.g., WhatsApp, FaceTime). As of March 31, 2026, this serves approximately 7.4 million monthly unique devices across ~30 countries.
*   **Future Capabilities:** SpaceX is developing comprehensive satellite-to-mobile services, including broadband data and IoT connectivity, which are expected to deliver resilient, infrastructure-independent connectivity worldwide and enable 5G.
*   **Partnerships & Revenue Model:** The company has partnerships with ~30 Mobile Network Operators (MNOs) on six continents, covering an area home to 1.9 billion people. SpaceX charges MNOs either a fixed fee or a per-mobile user fee, which is typically passed through to the end customer by the carrier.

### 4. Enterprise & Government Revenue
*   **Enterprise Solutions:** Starlink serves a wide array of industries (construction, aviation, maritime, retail, etc.) through a direct, vertical-specific sales model. Revenue is generated via contracts structured as subscriptions, data consumption, capacity, or percentage-of-completion.
*   **Government Solutions:** Revenue is generated via long-term contracts for "Starshield," a secure satellite network designed specifically for government entities.
*   **Financial Performance:** 
    *   In Q1 2026, enterprise and government revenue increased by $126 million year-over-year. This was driven by $209 million in growth from aviation, maritime, and other enterprise business, and $85 million in mobile connectivity, which partially offset a $175 million decrease in government connectivity.
    *   For the full year 2025, enterprise and government connectivity revenue increased by $1,411 million, driven by $1,218 million in enterprise growth (including $632 million in mobile connectivity) and $193 million in government growth.

### 5. Satellite Manufacturing & Network Scale
*   **Current Constellation:** As of March 31, 2026, SpaceX operates approximately 9,600 Starlink broadband and mobile satellites in Low-Earth Orbit (LEO), accounting for roughly 75% of all active maneuverable satellites in orbit.
*   **Next-Generation V3 Satellites:** SpaceX expects to commence deploying V3 satellites in the second half of 2026 using Starship. These satellites are designed to offer 1 Tbps of downlink capacity per satellite.
*   **Launch Efficiency:** A single Starship launch will be capable of deploying up to 60 V3 satellites to LEO, representing a potential twenty-fold increase in Starlink downlink capacity deployed relative to a Falcon 9 launch.
*   **Vertical Integration:** The company highlights its "highly verticalized supply chain" and in-house manufacturing as key drivers of scale, efficiency, and the compounding competitive advantage of its LEO constellations.

### 6. Forward-Looking Statements: Market Opportunity, Capacity, and Revenue Growth
*   **Total Addressable Market (TAM):** SpaceX estimates a $1.6 trillion total market opportunity across Starlink Broadband and Starlink Mobile, driven primarily by consumer use cases. Starlink Broadband alone represents an approximately $870 billion opportunity. The company expects the market to extend well beyond traditional definitions as connectivity becomes embedded in transportation networks, autonomous systems, and smart devices.
*   **Revenue Growth:** The Connectivity segment generated $11.4 billion in revenue in 2025 (up 49.8% YoY) and $3.26 billion in Q1 2026 (up 31.6% YoY). SpaceX expects to drive meaningful subscriber and revenue growth by expanding its retail distribution network, executing region-specific marketing, and demonstrating superior speed/affordability not just in rural areas, but also in suburban/urban regions with wireline broadband options.
*   **Capacity and Margin Expansion:** The deployment of V3 satellites via Starship will massively scale downlink capacity. The company expects to expand margins through operational efficiencies and technological advancements, driving strong sequential Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA growth. 
*   **Space Segment Synergy:** The filing notes that while the Space segment's revenue growth looks relatively lower because it does not recognize revenue for internal Starlink launches (costs are capitalized and depreciated within the Connectivity segment), this launch capacity provides "foundational strategic value" bolstering the growth of both Connectivity and the newly acquired AI segment.

steps.message: I now have comprehensive information on all three segments. Let me do one final calculation to annualize the Q1 2026 AI segment economics and verify the trajectory.
steps.reasoning_content:
I now have a comprehensive picture of all three segments. Let me also check for any additional context on the AI segment's customer concentration and the equity story framing, particularly around the AI segment's reliance on forward expectations.

Let me also verify the AI segment's revenue trajectory - it's interesting that revenue went from $2,961M in 2023 to $2,620M in 2024 to $3,201M in 2025. That's a decline then recovery. And the EBITDA went from positive ($1,222M in 2023, $347M in 2024) to negative ($-1,237M in 2025, $-609M in Q1 2026).

Wait - the AI segment includes X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025) and xAI (merged February 2, 2026). So the historical figures are recast to include both. The 2023 figure of $2,961M would be primarily X (Twitter) revenue since xAI was only founded in 2023. The 2024 figure of $2,620M would include X plus early xAI. The 2025 figure of $3,201M would include X plus more xAI revenue.

Actually, looking more carefully - the AI segment revenue is described as coming from "advertising, Grok and X subscriptions, and data licensing arrangements." So this is essentially the combined X + xAI business.

Now let me think about the equity story for each segment:

**Space Segment:**
- Revenue: $4,086M (FY2025), $619M (Q1 2026)
- Segment Adjusted EBITDA: $653M (FY2025), -$351M (Q1 2026)
- KPIs: 170 missions in 2025, 165 Falcon 9 launches, 11 of 12 NSSL missions, $13.7B NSSL Phase 3 contracts through 2032, 11 Starship flight tests
- Forward-looking: Starship commercialization (H2 2026), Mars, point-to-point travel, space tourism, in-orbit manufacturing
- The Space segment has substantial disclosed economics: $4B+ revenue, profitable EBITDA in 2025, government contracts providing visibility
- However, the future growth story (Starship, Mars) is heavily forward-looking

**Connectivity Segment:**
- Revenue: $11,387M (FY2025), $3,257M (Q1 2026)
- Segment Adjusted EBITDA: $7,168M (FY2025), $2,087M (Q1 2026)
- KPIs: 10.3M subscribers (Q1 2026), ARPU declining ($99→$66), 9,600 satellites, 7.4M mobile devices, $1.6T TAM
- Forward-looking: V3 satellites (H2 2026), 5G direct-to-cell, margin expansion
- The Connectivity segment has the strongest disclosed economics: $11B+ revenue, $7B+ EBITDA, growing subscribers, clear path to profitability
- This is the most mature and economically validated segment

**AI Segment:**
- Revenue: $3,201M (FY2025), $818M (Q1 2026)
- Segment Adjusted EBITDA: -$1,237M (FY2025), -$609M (Q1 2026)
- KPIs: 1.0 GW nameplate compute draw, COLOSSUS/COLOSSUS II data centers, Grok-4.3, 104M GPU market
- Forward-looking: AGI convergence, multi-trillion parameter models, orbital/lunar AI compute, "Everything App," enterprise/government adoption
- The AI segment has the weakest disclosed economics: revenue is essentially flat/declining (when adjusted for the X acquisition), EBITDA is deeply negative and worsening, massive CapEx ($7.7B in Q1 2026 alone)
- The equity story relies heavily on forward expectations: AGI, multi-trillion parameter models, orbital AI compute, lunar terawatt-scale compute

The AI segment's equity story relies most heavily on forward expectations rather than presently disclosed economics because:
1. Revenue is essentially flat ($2,961M → $2,620M → $3,201M from 2023-2025) - actually declined then recovered
2. EBITDA went from positive ($1,222M, $347M) to deeply negative (-$1,237M, -$609M annualized ~-$2.4B)
3. CapEx is enormous ($7.7B in Q1 2026 alone, $12.7B in FY2025)
4. The narrative depends on AGI convergence, multi-trillion parameter models, orbital/lunar compute, "Everything App" evolution
5. The market opportunity sizing (104M GPUs, $3.33/hour rental) is based on assumptions, not realized revenue
6. The segment is described as "newly acquired" and "earlier stage of development"
7. Customer concentration is not disclosed
8. The forward-looking statements about AGI, lunar terawatt-scale compute, and orbital AI infrastructure are highly speculative

Let me also verify the AI segment revenue trajectory and check if there's any additional context I'm missing. Let me also look at the consolidated financial highlights to confirm the figures.

Actually, I have enough information to answer the question. Let me also do a quick calculation to annualize the Q1 2026 AI EBITDA loss to show the trajectory.

Q1 2026 AI Segment Adjusted EBITDA: -$609M
Annualized: -$609M × 4 = -$2,436M

So the AI segment's EBITDA loss is accelerating: -$1,237M (FY2025) → -$2,436M (annualized Q1 2026)

Let me also calculate the AI segment's CapEx trajectory:
- FY2023: $463M
- FY2024: $5,633M
- FY2025: $12,727M
- Q1 2026: $7,723M (annualized: ~$30.9B)

This is a massive ramp in capital expenditure with deteriorating economics.

Now let me also think about the revenue trajectory more carefully. The AI segment includes X (Twitter) which was acquired by xAI in March 2025, and xAI was acquired by SpaceX in February 2026. The recast financials include both for all periods.

X (Twitter) revenue was around $3-4B annually before the acquisition. xAI revenue was minimal in 2023 (Grok-1 launched November 2023). So:
- 2023: $2,961M (mostly X advertising, which had declined post-acquisition)
- 2024: $2,620M (X advertising continued to decline)
- 2025: $3,201M (X + growing xAI subscriptions/data licensing)

The revenue recovery in 2025 is driven by X and Grok subscriptions, but the segment is now burning massive cash on compute infrastructure.

Let me now structure my answer. I'll provide:
1. A summary of each segment's investment narrative based on disclosed KPIs, revenue, and Segment Adjusted EBITDA
2. An analysis of which segment relies most heavily on forward expectations

The answer is clearly the AI segment. Let me write this up.

Actually, let me do one more retrieval to make sure I have the complete picture on the AI segment's revenue composition and any additional forward-looking statements.

arguments.final_result:
## Investment Narrative by Segment — SpaceX S-1 (Filed May 20, 2026)

All figures are from the retrospectively recast consolidated financial statements, which include xAI (merged Feb 2, 2026) and X Holdings (merged into xAI Mar 28, 2025) for all historical periods presented. No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations.

---

### 1. SPACE SEGMENT — "Launch Monopoly Funding the Starship Option"

**Disclosed economics (presently validated):**
| Metric | FY2023 | FY2024 | FY2025 | Q1 2026 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Segment Revenue | $3,557M | $3,796M | $4,086M | $619M |
| Segment Adj. EBITDA | n/d | $1,154M¹ | $653M | $(351)M |
| Starship R&D | — | — | $3,004M | $930M |

¹ Implied from disclosed YoY decline of $501M.

**Disclosed KPIs:**
- ~650 orbital launches cumulative; 170 missions in 2025 (165 Falcon 9, 157 flight-proven)
- 11 of 12 NSSL medium/heavy missions in 2025; all 5 NASA ISS crew/cargo missions
- ~$13.7B awarded across NSSL Phase 3 Lane 2 through 2032 (~54 missions); Phase 3 manifest = 84 missions (nearly 2× Phase 2)
- >80% of global mass-to-orbit in 2025; >99% Falcon 9 mission success rate
- 11 Starship flight tests completed; 12th scheduled

**Equity story:** A profitable, government-anchored launch monopoly (NSSL, NASA CRS, DoD) generating ~$4B revenue and positive EBITDA in 2025, with the option value of Starship commercialization (payload delivery expected H2 2026) and long-dated TAM expansion (point-to-point Earth travel, space tourism, in-orbit manufacturing, multiplanetary transport). The present economics are real and contracted; the upside is forward-looking but anchored to a flight-tested vehicle and a $13.7B contracted backlog.

---

### 2. CONNECTIVITY SEGMENT — "The Cash Engine"

**Disclosed economics (presently validated):**
| Metric | FY2023 | FY2024 | FY2025 | Q1 2026 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Segment Revenue | $3,869M | $7,599M | $11,387M | $3,257M |
| Segment Adj. EBITDA | $1,602M | $3,849M | $7,168M | $2,087M |
| YoY Revenue Growth | — | +96.4% | +49.8% | +31.6% |

**Disclosed KPIs:**
- Starlink subscribers: 2.3M (2023) → 4.4M (2024) → 8.9M (2025) → 10.3M (Q1 2026); +105% YoY in Q1 2026
- ARPU declining by design: $99 (2023) → $91 (2024) → $81 (2025) → $66 (Q1 2026), driven by international expansion and lower-priced plans
- ~9,600 Starlink broadband/mobile satellites in LEO (~75% of all active maneuverable satellites)
- Direct-to-Cell: ~650 V1 Mobile satellites; 7.4M monthly unique devices across ~30 countries; partnerships with ~30 MNOs covering 1.9B people
- Enterprise + government revenue +$1,411M YoY in 2025 (incl. $632M mobile connectivity growth)
- Disclosed TAM: ~$1.6T (Starlink Broadband ~$870B + Starlink Mobile)

**Equity story:** The most economically validated segment. $11.4B revenue growing ~50% with $7.2B Segment Adjusted EBITDA (63% EBITDA margin) and a clear KPI flywheel (subscribers, satellites, mobile devices, MNO partnerships). ARPU decline is a disclosed strategic choice to drive volume. Forward-looking upside (V3 satellites at 1 Tbps each via Starship, 5G direct-to-cell, margin expansion) is layered on top of already-profitable, scaling economics.

---

### 3. AI SEGMENT — "Compute as the Next Trillion-Dollar Market"

**Disclosed economics (presently validated):**
| Metric | FY2023 | FY2024 | FY2025 | Q1 2026 | Q1 2026 Annualized |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Segment Revenue | $2,961M | $2,620M | $3,201M | $818M | ~$3,272M |
| Segment Adj. EBITDA | $1,222M | $347M | $(1,237)M | $(609)M | ~$(2,436)M |
| Loss from Operations | $(3,973)M | $(1,561)M | $(6,355)M | $(2,469)M | ~$(9,876)M |
| Nameplate Compute Draw | 0 GW | 0.3 GW | 0.8 GW | 1.0 GW | — |
| CapEx | $463M | $5,633M | $12,727M | $7,723M | ~$30,892M |

**Disclosed KPIs:**
- 1.0 GW nameplate compute draw (COLOSSUS + COLOSSUS II online); COLOSSUS built in 122 days, COLOSSUS II in 91 days (vs. ~2-year industry benchmark for 100 MW greenfield)
- Grok model family: Grok-1 (Nov 2023) → Grok-4.3 (Apr 2026); Grok 5 in development
- Frontier-level GPQA Diamond score within 2 years of first release
- Revenue streams: X/Grok subscriptions, advertising, data licensing, Grok API/Business/Enterprise/Voice, Imagine
- Market sizing: 104M GPUs required at 80% utilization and $3.33/hour rental (per Silicon Data)

**Equity story:** The narrative is almost entirely forward-looking. Revenue is essentially flat over 2 years (+8.1% cumulative from $2,961M to $3,201M), and the segment flipped from $1.2B positive EBITDA in 2023 to a ~$2.4B annualized EBITDA loss in Q1 2026 — a ~$3.6B swing — while CapEx annualized to ~$30.9B. The disclosed value proposition rests on: (i) AGI convergence ("AI is rapidly converging toward AGI"), (ii) multi-trillion parameter models representing "a step change in reasoning depth," (iii) orbital and lunar AI compute ("terawatt-scale annual AI compute growth" if a lunar presence is achieved), (iv) X evolving into an "Everything App" (payments, banking, commerce), and (v) growing enterprise/government adoption (unquantified). Customer concentration is not disclosed.

---

## Which Segment's Equity Story Relies Most Heavily on Forward Expectations?

**The AI segment.**

The contrast across the three segments is stark when measured against presently disclosed economics:

| Dimension | Space | Connectivity | AI |
|---|---|---|---|
| FY2025 Revenue | $4.1B | $11.4B | $3.2B |
| FY2025 Segment Adj. EBITDA | $653M | $7,168M | $(1,237)M |
| EBITDA Trend | Profitable, declining on Starship R&D | Profitable and expanding | Swung from +$1.2B to negative; worsening |
| Revenue Growth (2-yr) | +14.9% | +194% | +8.1% |
| CapEx Intensity (FY2025) | $3.8B | $4.2B | $12.7B |
| Contracted Backlog / Validated KPIs | $13.7B NSSL Phase 3; 170 missions/yr | 10.3M subs; 9,600 sats; 7.4M mobile devices | 1.0 GW compute; Grok-4.3 (no revenue/customer KPIs tied to compute) |
| Forward-Looking Pillars | Starship commercialization (H2 2026); Mars | V3 satellites; 5G D2C; margin expansion | AGI; multi-trillion-param models; orbital/lunar terawatt compute; "Everything App" |

**Why AI relies most on forward expectations:**

1. **Deteriorating present economics.** Segment Adjusted EBITDA moved from +$1,222M (2023) → +$347M (2024) → −$1,237M (2025) → −$609M in Q1 2026 alone (≈ −$2.4B annualized). The filing attributes this to the segment's "earlier stage of development and continued investments to support long-term growth opportunities in AI" — explicitly framing current losses as a forward bet.

2. **Revenue is not validating the spend.** Despite $12.7B of CapEx in 2025 and ~$30.9B annualized in Q1 2026, revenue grew only ~8% cumulatively over two years and is essentially flat on a Q1-annualized basis. The compute ramp (0 → 1.0 GW) is not yet translating into proportional revenue.

3. **The TAM and unit economics are assumption-driven, not realized.** The 104M-GPU / $3.33-per-hour / 80%-utilization market sizing is sourced to third parties (Silicon Data, National Electrical Installation Standards) and described as subject to market conditions — not a disclosed customer book or contracted revenue.

4. **The strategic value propositions are aspirational.** AGI convergence, multi-trillion-parameter "step change," orbital AI compute, lunar "terawatt-scale annual AI compute growth," and X as an "Everything App" are roadmap/mission statements, not presently disclosed economics. Several depend on Starship success (itself a forward-looking milestone).

5. **Customer concentration and monetization metrics are absent.** Unlike Connectivity (subscribers, ARPU, MNO partners) and Space (NSSL backlog, mission count), the AI segment discloses no customer count, no API revenue, no enterprise/government adoption metrics, and no unit economics for Grok subscriptions.

By contrast, **Connectivity** is the most economically validated (profitable, scaling, KPI-rich), and **Space** sits in the middle — profitable today with a large contracted government backlog, but with its largest upside (Starship, Mars) still forward-looking. The **AI segment** is the only one where the equity story is predominantly a forward-expectations narrative layered on presently negative and worsening economics.

---

### Sources

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm",
      "name": "Space Exploration Technologies Corp. (SpaceX) Form S-1, filed May 20, 2026 — Segment financials, KPIs, MD&A, and forward-looking disclosures for Space, Connectivity, and AI segments"
    }
  ]
}

## Investment Narrative by Segment — SpaceX S-1 (Filed May 20, 2026)

All figures are from the retrospectively recast consolidated financial statements, which include xAI (merged Feb 2, 2026) and X Holdings (merged into xAI Mar 28, 2025) for all historical periods presented. No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations.

---

### 1. SPACE SEGMENT — "Launch Monopoly Funding the Starship Option"

**Disclosed economics (presently validated):**
| Metric | FY2023 | FY2024 | FY2025 | Q1 2026 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Segment Revenue | $3,557M | $3,796M | $4,086M | $619M |
| Segment Adj. EBITDA | n/d | $1,154M¹ | $653M | $(351)M |
| Starship R&D | — | — | $3,004M | $930M |

¹ Implied from disclosed YoY decline of $501M.

**Disclosed KPIs:**
- ~650 orbital launches cumulative; 170 missions in 2025 (165 Falcon 9, 157 flight-proven)
- 11 of 12 NSSL medium/heavy missions in 2025; all 5 NASA ISS crew/cargo missions
- ~$13.7B awarded across NSSL Phase 3 Lane 2 through 2032 (~54 missions); Phase 3 manifest = 84 missions (nearly 2× Phase 2)
- >80% of global mass-to-orbit in 2025; >99% Falcon 9 mission success rate
- 11 Starship flight tests completed; 12th scheduled

**Equity story:** A profitable, government-anchored launch monopoly (NSSL, NASA CRS, DoD) generating ~$4B revenue and positive EBITDA in 2025, with the option value of Starship commercialization (payload delivery expected H2 2026) and long-dated TAM expansion (point-to-point Earth travel, space tourism, in-orbit manufacturing, multiplanetary transport). The present economics are real and contracted; the upside is forward-looking but anchored to a flight-tested vehicle and a $13.7B contracted backlog.

---

### 2. CONNECTIVITY SEGMENT — "The Cash Engine"

**Disclosed economics (presently validated):**
| Metric | FY2023 | FY2024 | FY2025 | Q1 2026 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Segment Revenue | $3,869M | $7,599M | $11,387M | $3,257M |
| Segment Adj. EBITDA | $1,602M | $3,849M | $7,168M | $2,087M |
| YoY Revenue Growth | — | +96.4% | +49.8% | +31.6% |

**Disclosed KPIs:**
- Starlink subscribers: 2.3M (2023) → 4.4M (2024) → 8.9M (2025) → 10.3M (Q1 2026); +105% YoY in Q1 2026
- ARPU declining by design: $99 (2023) → $91 (2024) → $81 (2025) → $66 (Q1 2026), driven by international expansion and lower-priced plans
- ~9,600 Starlink broadband/mobile satellites in LEO (~75% of all active maneuverable satellites)
- Direct-to-Cell: ~650 V1 Mobile satellites; 7.4M monthly unique devices across ~30 countries; partnerships with ~30 MNOs covering 1.9B people
- Enterprise + government revenue +$1,411M YoY in 2025 (incl. $632M mobile connectivity growth)
- Disclosed TAM: ~$1.6T (Starlink Broadband ~$870B + Starlink Mobile)

**Equity story:** The most economically validated segment. $11.4B revenue growing ~50% with $7.2B Segment Adjusted EBITDA (63% EBITDA margin) and a clear KPI flywheel (subscribers, satellites, mobile devices, MNO partnerships). ARPU decline is a disclosed strategic choice to drive volume. Forward-looking upside (V3 satellites at 1 Tbps each via Starship, 5G direct-to-cell, margin expansion) is layered on top of already-profitable, scaling economics.

---

### 3. AI SEGMENT — "Compute as the Next Trillion-Dollar Market"

**Disclosed economics (presently validated):**
| Metric | FY2023 | FY2024 | FY2025 | Q1 2026 | Q1 2026 Annualized |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Segment Revenue | $2,961M | $2,620M | $3,201M | $818M | ~$3,272M |
| Segment Adj. EBITDA | $1,222M | $347M | $(1,237)M | $(609)M | ~$(2,436)M |
| Loss from Operations | $(3,973)M | $(1,561)M | $(6,355)M | $(2,469)M | ~$(9,876)M |
| Nameplate Compute Draw | 0 GW | 0.3 GW | 0.8 GW | 1.0 GW | — |
| CapEx | $463M | $5,633M | $12,727M | $7,723M | ~$30,892M |

**Disclosed KPIs:**
- 1.0 GW nameplate compute draw (COLOSSUS + COLOSSUS II online); COLOSSUS built in 122 days, COLOSSUS II in 91 days (vs. ~2-year industry benchmark for 100 MW greenfield)
- Grok model family: Grok-1 (Nov 2023) → Grok-4.3 (Apr 2026); Grok 5 in development
- Frontier-level GPQA Diamond score within 2 years of first release
- Revenue streams: X/Grok subscriptions, advertising, data licensing, Grok API/Business/Enterprise/Voice, Imagine
- Market sizing: 104M GPUs required at 80% utilization and $3.33/hour rental (per Silicon Data)

**Equity story:** The narrative is almost entirely forward-looking. Revenue is essentially flat over 2 years (+8.1% cumulative from $2,961M to $3,201M), and the segment flipped from $1.2B positive EBITDA in 2023 to a ~$2.4B annualized EBITDA loss in Q1 2026 — a ~$3.6B swing — while CapEx annualized to ~$30.9B. The disclosed value proposition rests on: (i) AGI convergence ("AI is rapidly converging toward AGI"), (ii) multi-trillion parameter models representing "a step change in reasoning depth," (iii) orbital and lunar AI compute ("terawatt-scale annual AI compute growth" if a lunar presence is achieved), (iv) X evolving into an "Everything App" (payments, banking, commerce), and (v) growing enterprise/government adoption (unquantified). Customer concentration is not disclosed.

---

## Which Segment's Equity Story Relies Most Heavily on Forward Expectations?

**The AI segment.**

The contrast across the three segments is stark when measured against presently disclosed economics:

| Dimension | Space | Connectivity | AI |
|---|---|---|---|
| FY2025 Revenue | $4.1B | $11.4B | $3.2B |
| FY2025 Segment Adj. EBITDA | $653M | $7,168M | $(1,237)M |
| EBITDA Trend | Profitable, declining on Starship R&D | Profitable and expanding | Swung from +$1.2B to negative; worsening |
| Revenue Growth (2-yr) | +14.9% | +194% | +8.1% |
| CapEx Intensity (FY2025) | $3.8B | $4.2B | $12.7B |
| Contracted Backlog / Validated KPIs | $13.7B NSSL Phase 3; 170 missions/yr | 10.3M subs; 9,600 sats; 7.4M mobile devices | 1.0 GW compute; Grok-4.3 (no revenue/customer KPIs tied to compute) |
| Forward-Looking Pillars | Starship commercialization (H2 2026); Mars | V3 satellites; 5G D2C; margin expansion | AGI; multi-trillion-param models; orbital/lunar terawatt compute; "Everything App" |

**Why AI relies most on forward expectations:**

1. **Deteriorating present economics.** Segment Adjusted EBITDA moved from +$1,222M (2023) → +$347M (2024) → −$1,237M (2025) → −$609M in Q1 2026 alone (≈ −$2.4B annualized). The filing attributes this to the segment's "earlier stage of development and continued investments to support long-term growth opportunities in AI" — explicitly framing current losses as a forward bet.

2. **Revenue is not validating the spend.** Despite $12.7B of CapEx in 2025 and ~$30.9B annualized in Q1 2026, revenue grew only ~8% cumulatively over two years and is essentially flat on a Q1-annualized basis. The compute ramp (0 → 1.0 GW) is not yet translating into proportional revenue.

3. **The TAM and unit economics are assumption-driven, not realized.** The 104M-GPU / $3.33-per-hour / 80%-utilization market sizing is sourced to third parties (Silicon Data, National Electrical Installation Standards) and described as subject to market conditions — not a disclosed customer book or contracted revenue.

4. **The strategic value propositions are aspirational.** AGI convergence, multi-trillion-parameter "step change," orbital AI compute, lunar "terawatt-scale annual AI compute growth," and X as an "Everything App" are roadmap/mission statements, not presently disclosed economics. Several depend on Starship success (itself a forward-looking milestone).

5. **Customer concentration and monetization metrics are absent.** Unlike Connectivity (subscribers, ARPU, MNO partners) and Space (NSSL backlog, mission count), the AI segment discloses no customer count, no API revenue, no enterprise/government adoption metrics, and no unit economics for Grok subscriptions.

By contrast, **Connectivity** is the most economically validated (profitable, scaling, KPI-rich), and **Space** sits in the middle — profitable today with a large contracted government backlog, but with its largest upside (Starship, Mars) still forward-looking. The **AI segment** is the only one where the equity story is predominantly a forward-expectations narrative layered on presently negative and worsening economics.
